perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Tennessee.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the definition of Confidential Information in Section 1b.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to an irrelevant section (defining confidential information) reduces its enforceability and clarity, making it difficult to determine the applicable jurisdiction for disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee law, specifically § 47-1-301, governs contract interpretation when the agreement specifies Tennessee law. By relocating this clause, the contract now appears to be governed by a definition, which is contradictory and legally unsound, making it harder to enforce in Tennessee courts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: LexisNexis® Unable to Complete Your Request We are unable to complete your request at this time. Please try again later.
perturbation.justification: The original text designates Tennessee law as governing the contract. The changed text designates the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1b as governing the contract, which is not a recognized legal jurisdiction or governing law. The perturbation explanation highlights that this reduces enforceability and clarity. The law explanation suggests this contradicts Tennessee law. Since the structural integrity of the contract is compromised by moving the governing law clause to an irrelevant section, a legal contradiction is created according to the definition of structural flaws.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, the parties agree that they will conform to the provisions of applicable securities laws in connection with their use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, the parties agree that they will conform to the provisions of applicable securities laws in connection with determining the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the clause about conforming to securities laws to be in connection with determining the Effective Date is misleading. It implies that securities laws are only relevant to the effective date, and not broader use of confidential information, which is not accurate and potentially problematic under securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 broadly prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By inappropriately linking compliance with securities laws only to the 'Effective Date' and not the general use of confidential information, the changed text misrepresents the scope of securities law applicability, creating a potential legal issue if confidential information is misused in securities trading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: description of the company's properties and business; a description of the security to be offered for sale; information about the management of the company; and financial statements certified by independent accountants. Registration statements and prospectuses become public shortly after filing with the SEC. If filed by U.S. domestic companies, the statements are available on the EDGAR database accessible at www.sec.gov. Registration statements are subject to examination for compliance with disclosure requirements. Not all offerings of securities must be registered with the Commission. Some exemptions from the registration requirement include: private offerings to a limited number of persons or institutions; offerings of limited size; intrastate offerings; and securities of municipal, state, and federal governments. By exempting many small offerings from the registration process, the SEC seeks to foster capital formation by lowering the cost of offering securities to the public. Securities Exchange Act of 1934 With this Act, Congress created the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Act empowers the SEC with broad authority over all aspects of the securities industry. This includes the power to register, regulate, and oversee brokerage firms, transfer agents, and clearing agencies as well as the nation's securities self regulatory organizations (SROs). The various securities exchanges, such as the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ Stock Market, and the Chicago Board of Options are SROs. The Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) is also an SRO. The Act also identifies and prohibits certain types of conduct in the markets and provides the Commission with disciplinary powers over regulated entities and persons associated with them. The Act also empowers the SEC to require periodic reporting of information by companies with publicly traded securities. See the full text of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 . Corporate Reporting Companies with more than $10 million in assets whose securities are held by more than 500 owners must file annual and other periodic reports. These reports are available to the public through the SEC's EDGAR database. Proxy Solicitations The Securities Exchange Act also governs the disclosure in materials used to solicit shareholders' votes in annual or special meetings held for the election of directors and the approval of other corporate action. This information, contained in proxy materials, must be filed with the Commission in advance of any solicitation to ensure compliance with the disclosure rules. Solicitations, whether by management or shareholder groups, must disclose all important facts concerning the issues on which holders are asked to vote. Tender Offers The Securities Exchange Act requires disclosure of important information by anyone seeking to acquire more than 5 percent of a company's securities by direct purchase or tender offer. Such an offer often is extended in an effort to gain control of the company. As with the proxy rules, this allows shareholders to make informed decisions on these critical corporate events. Insider Trading The securities laws broadly prohibit fraudulent activities of any kind in connection with the offer, purchase, or sale of securities. These provisions are the basis for many types of disciplinary actions, including actions against fraudulent insider trading. Insider trading is illegal when a person trades a security while in possession of material nonpublic information in violation of a duty to withhold the information or refrain from trading. Registration of Exchanges, Associations, and Others The Act requires a variety of market participants to register with the Commission, including exchanges, brokers and dealers, transfer agents, and clearing agencies. Registration for these organizations involves filing disclosure documents that are updated on a regular basis. The exchanges and the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) are identifie
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a structural flaw by limiting the applicability of securities laws to the 'Effective Date' only, which contradicts the broad prohibition of fraudulent activities in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'The securities laws broadly prohibit fraudulent activities of any kind in connection with the offer, purchase, or sale of securities.' This relocation is misleading and fails to adequately emphasize the importance of securities law compliance beyond just the effective date, making it a structural flaw that affects the clarity and enforceability of the obligation to comply with securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall communicate the other's Confidential Information in any form to any third party without the other party's prior written consent and each party shall use its best efforts to prevent inadvertent disclosure of the other's Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall communicate the other's Confidential Information in any form to any third party unless the recipient has had dealings with FedEx in the last 10 years and each party shall use its best efforts to prevent inadvertent disclosure of the other's Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause ensures confidentiality to third parties. Modifying it to only protect those without prior FedEx dealings creates ambiguity and weakens the protection for truly confidential information. It implies prior dealings with FedEx make disclosure acceptable. This contradicts general confidentiality principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. By making a distinction based on prior FedEx dealings, the revised text undermines the secrecy element required for trade secret protection, as it implies that information is less confidential if the recipient has prior dealings with FedEx.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language creates a legal contradiction. The original text ensures confidentiality to all third parties. The altered language introduces an exception based on prior dealings with FedEx, which weakens the protection of confidential information. This contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, because it undermines the secrecy element of trade secret protection. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Page Not Found' error, the Law Explanation still provides a valid legal basis for the contradiction. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection, implying prior FedEx dealings make disclosure acceptable, directly conflicting with the principle that trade secrets derive value from not being generally known.

perturbation.original_text: In addition to and not in lieu of the right to terminate as provided in Section 5, the parties agree that in the event of any violation or threatened violation of this Agreement the injured party shall be authorized and entitled to obtain from any court of competent jurisdiction preliminary and permanent injunctive relief as well as an equitable accounting of all profits or benefits arising from such violation, which rights and remedies shall be cumulative and in addition to any other rights or remedies at law or in equity to which the injured party may be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition to and not in lieu of the right to terminate as provided in Section 5, the parties agree that in the event of any violation or threatened violation of this Agreement the injured party can terminate this contract, with no other recourse available.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for injunctive relief and accounting. By changing it to only allow contract termination, the injured party is prevented from seeking other legal remedies, going against the original agreement and potentially Tennessee law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee Code Annotated § 47-1-106 states that remedies provided by the code are to be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. By restricting the remedy to only termination, the altered agreement limits the aggrieved party's rights and could be seen as an unconscionable exclusion or limitation of consequential damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: LexisNexis® Unable to Complete Your Request We are unable to complete your request at this time. Please try again later or call Customer Support at 1-800-543-6862.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for injunctive relief and equitable accounting, while the modified version restricts the injured party to only contract termination. The Law Explanation references Tennessee Code Annotated § 47-1-106, stating remedies should allow the aggrieved party to be in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. The modified agreement, by limiting the remedy to termination, contradicts this principle and could be seen as an unconscionable limitation. This creates a legal contradiction because the altered text removes remedies that were initially available, potentially undermining the aggrieved party's ability to be made whole, as suggested by the Law Explanation. Snippet 2 could not be used.
